weather: 3°F at first light, steadying to 7°F by noon. Sky clear and high and very blue, the particular blue that comes after the cold has finished making its point and is simply residing now. No wind. The lake sealed and white. Smoke from every chimney bending slightly south — the first wind in four days, too gentle to be called a wind, more a suggestion.

lead.headline: The Box Holds Thirteen Items and Is Not Waiting for Anything; the Town Sits With This for a Moment and Finds It Sufficient
lead.body:
On the morning of Thursday, November the thirteenth, the lost-and-found box at the Greywater Gazette held thirteen items and received no new ones, and the town of Greywater Falls conducted itself accordingly — which is to say warmly, practically, and with the slightly dazed quality of a place that has been leaning into a wind that has just, quietly, stopped. The second T. Aldercott has been here for twenty-four hours. Calloway has been here for four days. Doreen Halloway served twelve Standings at the counter this morning, and this editor ate one of them standing up at the Kettle counter next to Calloway, who was reading something in a small green notebook, and T. Aldercott, who had her typewriter out and was typing in the unhurried way of someone who has arrived at the thing they came to type, and none of it — the coffee, the pastry, the mechanical clatter of the typewriter, the blue-bordered plate on the counter between them — none of it felt strange. That is either the accommodation the town has always known how to make, or it is something this editor does not have a word for yet. Both, she suspects.

Calloway came to the Gazette at ten. She is a librarian, and librarians, in this editor's experience, are the only people who approach an archive the way it deserves to be approached: without hurry, without agenda, with the patient belief that the thing you are looking for is probably there and will show itself when you are ready to see it. She sat with the lost-and-found box for forty minutes. She did not remove any items. She made a list in her green notebook, in a handwriting that this editor — who could not help but notice, standing at her own desk across the room — recognised as organized and careful. At the end of forty minutes she closed the notebook and said: 'It's a complete correspondence.' This editor asked: complete as in finished, or complete as in whole. Calloway thought about it for a moment and said: 'I think both. I think that's the same thing here.' She left the box exactly as she found it. She left the Gazette carrying the green notebook and the canvas bag and nothing else, and she walked toward the Kettle, and this editor watched her go, and then looked at the box for a long time and then looked away, which is the correct response and always has been.

Gerald Pith was at the lake shore at seven this morning — not to measure, but to stand. He has measured seventeen and five-eighths inches, and then the rate slowed, and now the lake is ice from shore to shore and the measurement cannot be continued and this appears to have freed him from something. He stood at the ice-line with his hands in his coat pockets and no notebook visible for the first time in thirty-nine days. Russ Dunmore drove past at seven-fifteen on his morning rounds and slowed the cruiser and looked at Gerald standing there, and Gerald looked back, and Russ pulled over and got out and stood next to him, and the two of them stood at the ice together for a while, saying nothing official. Russ told this editor about it later, at the Kettle, in the tone he uses when a thing has been added to the record: 'Nothing to file,' he said. 'Just the lake.' He drank his coffee. He looked like a man who had been carrying something heavy for a long season and had set it down somewhere reasonable.

At dusk the returned gathered at the ice-line — Mr. Whiskers, the drake, the rabbit, the sparrow, cold and gentle and content — and they hummed the held low note into the darkening air. The hum lasted six minutes and twelve seconds, which is the shortest it has been. Toby Fern timed it. Agnes Crewe was on her step, not watching the lake but watching the sky, in the posture of a woman who has already watched the lake and knows what the sky does afterward. T. Aldercott stood on the bank with her hands in her pockets, and she did not look like someone who had arrived; she looked like someone who had been here long enough to stand at the water the way you stand at something that is yours — not owned, but known. The hum dropped away at six minutes and twelve seconds and the air was very clear and very still, and this editor wrote down the time and the duration and filed them in the second notebook, and then she went inside and moved the cups from the shelf to the table, which took eleven seconds and which Agnes Crewe told her to do three days ago, and which she has been ready to do ever since, and which is, she has decided, enough.

briefs.headline: LAKE WATCH, Day 20 — Level: 82; Temp: 3°F; Clarity: Exceptional, Visible to Thirty-One Feet; Sign Count: 7; Returned: Mr. Whiskers, Drake, Rabbit, Sparrow — Hummed at the Ice-Line Six Minutes Twelve Seconds, the Briefest Yet; the Rowboat at Thirty-One Feet Remains Settled; Its Shadow Has Begun to Fall Correctly
briefs.body: Filed by Toby Fern, Lake Watch correspondent. The hum tonight was six minutes and twelve seconds, down from nine forty Wednesday. I timed it on my phone with the stopwatch app. The rowboat's shadow — which has been falling wrong since Day 14 — was falling correctly at dusk tonight, by which I mean it pointed the way a shadow points when the light is ordinary and above you, not below you. I wrote this in the Watch notebook and then I wrote it in the second notebook because I wasn't sure which kind of fact it was. Agnes Crewe, when I told her, said: 'Things that have been down a long time eventually remember which way is up.' I asked if that was good. She said: 'It's accurate.' I'm putting that in both notebooks too.

briefs.headline: Gerald Pith Stands at the Ice Without the Notebook; Russ Dunmore Stops the Cruiser; Both Men Appear to Find This Arrangement Satisfactory
briefs.body: Gerald Pith was observed at the lake shore at seven this morning without the blue notebook, standing with his hands in his coat pockets and no apparent measuring apparatus. Constable Dunmore stopped his patrol cruiser and got out and stood alongside him. Duration of the standing: approximately twelve minutes, by this editor's estimate from the Gazette window. Gerald Pith, when reached for comment at the Kettle, said: 'The ice has it now. The ice will tell me what it tells me.' He had his coffee. He did not write anything in the margin of anything. This paper considers this a significant development and will continue to monitor.

briefs.headline: Calloway Inventories the Lost-and-Found Box; Calls It a Complete Correspondence; Returns It to Its Shelf Undisturbed; the Town Finds This the Correct Conclusion
briefs.body: Calloway, who has been in Greywater Falls since Monday and who appears to have settled in with the competent ease of someone who has moved before and knows which things matter first, sat with the Gazette's lost-and-found box for forty minutes this morning and produced a handwritten list of its contents. She has not shared the list. She called the box 'a complete correspondence' and noted that it appeared to be 'both finished and whole, which here is the same thing.' The box remains on the shelf at thirteen items. No new items have been placed in it since Wednesday. The Gazette's official lost-and-found column will return to its regular alphabetical format beginning next week.

briefs.headline: T. Aldercott Types at the Kettle Counter for Two Hours; Marigold Refills the Cup Twice Without Interrupting; Doreen Halloway Says This Is What the Table Is For
briefs.body: T. Aldercott set up her typewriter at the Kettle counter Thursday morning and worked for approximately two hours. The typewriter is a manual model, make undisclosed, and its sound, Marigold Vance reports, was 'extremely satisfying, like the counter needed it.' Doreen Halloway came by at ten with a fresh plate of Standings and said, upon seeing the typewriter, 'well, that's what a table's for,' which was understood by everyone present as an unusually direct statement from a woman who communicates primarily through baked goods and placement of said baked goods. The Standings were eaten. The typing continued.

classifieds: FOUND — one season's worth of unasked questions, filed in good order and no longer urgent. Unclaimed items may remain where they are. They are not in the way. — The Greywater Gazette Lost-and-Found

FOR RENT — upstairs room, Halloway house, lake-road side. Warm. Good window. Currently occupied. Second room available if the occasion calls for it. Inquire at the bakery. Bring a reasonable appetite. — D. Halloway
WANTED — one sturdy outdoor chair, suitable for a step, suitable for winter sitting, in blue or near-blue. Not urgent. Just thinking ahead. — A. Crewe, Cottage, Lakeside

NOTICE — Mossley Hardware will host its annual Winterization Clinic on Saturday the 15th from nine to noon. Weatherstripping, pipe-wrap, and storm-window consulting available. Coffee will be on. This is not a vigil. It is, however, a good reason to come in. — H. Mossley
record: No new items placed in the Gazette lost-and-found, Thursday, November the 13th. Box stands at thirteen. Channel considered closed in good order.
Hum duration at dusk, Thursday, November the 13th: six minutes, twelve seconds. Recorded by T. Fern, Lake Watch Day 20. Previous: nine minutes, forty seconds.

letters.from: Doreen Halloway, Halloway's Bakery, Main Square
letters.body: Now, I want to say — and I want it in the paper, so there's no confusion later — that the upstairs room at my house is very comfortable and the linen is the good linen and I have put extra blankets in both closets because the lake-road side gets a draught from the north that is not serious but is present, and I have been aware of it for years, and I have put the draught stopper at the base of the door, and if either of the guests finds they are cold in the night they should knock on the hall door because I am a light sleeper and I don't mind. I have been a light sleeper since — well. Since. Anyway. The Standings this morning were the cardamom ones, which I make when it seems right, and it seemed right. That's all I wanted to say.

letters.from: Russ Dunmore, Greywater Falls Constabulary, Main Square
letters.body: For the record, and for the paper, and because I believe in the paper even when I am not entirely easy about what the paper prints: I stood at the lake this morning for approximately twelve minutes with Gerald Pith, who did not have his notebook, and I did not have my forms, and the lake was frozen and quiet and — this is the part I am including for the record and not for any other reason — it looked, this morning, in the early light, like nothing so much as the lake. Like something that is exactly what it appears to be and is doing exactly what it came to do. I have filed this observation as a personal note rather than an official report, because it did not require a form. That is a new category for me. I wanted to note that.

editorNote: This paper has been publishing for eleven years. In eleven years this editor has not missed a Thursday. The lost-and-found column ran first on page three; it moved to page two when the items grew interesting; it is on the front page now, and it will stay there until it isn't, and then it will go back to page three, and the town will go on. That is what the town does. That is the whole of it.
